danger_signals.visual_form: character entrance animation and stance change
danger_signals.meaning: match begins; opponent is now a threat

danger_signals.visual_form: character performs stylized special attack animation
danger_signals.meaning: opponent using high-damage technique; immediate danger

safety_signals.visual_form: opponent defeat animation and match end screen

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: characters opposing Ryo Sakazaki and Robert Garcia
Mr. Big as primary antagonist; associated minions as stage opponents

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: character roster expansion across sequels; new fighting stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: introduction of new opponent mechanics and fighting styles

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Yuri Sakazaki's absence from playable roster in first game
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ryo and Robert driven to face increasingly dangerous opponents to rescue Yuri

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Art of Fighting 2 tournament setting; multiple antagonistic factions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ryo and friends cooperate against shared mafia threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Art of Fighting 3 introduces motion capture sprite work; enhanced animation quality
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: shift from Sakazaki family story to Robert Garcia quest narrative

environmental_storytelling.example: Ryo and Robert Garcia are only playable heroes in single-player story mode of first game; other fighters available only in versus mode, signaling their narrative primacy
Art of Fighting 2 retains most characters from first game while adding new fighters, showing world expansion and broadened conflict
Mr. Big and Mr. Karate require specific conditions (second player join in arcade, cheat codes in home version) to play, marking them as secret/elite enemies
Art of Fighting 3 employs motion capture technology with advanced sprite-work, visually communicating technological progression and narrative sophistication

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: serves as climactic opponent; cliff-hanger reveal that he is Takuma Sakazaki signals hidden identity mechanic
pursues opponents through progression to rescue kidnapped sister; behavior consistent across sequels
fights alongside Ryo in early games; becomes narrative focus in Art of Fighting 3 with independent quest motivation
Art of Fighting 2 reveals he orchestrated first game's events; signifies conspiracy unfolding across game narrative

notes: Art of Fighting communicates world-state primarily through narrative progression and roster changes rather than environmental visual design. The fighting game paradigm limits real-time world-state visualization; changes manifest as story beats between matches. The series employs character introduction and relationship shifts to communicate political upheaval (Mr. Big's criminal operation, Geese Howard's mafia conspiracy). Visual communication relies on character sprite selection, opponent lineup progression, and cutscene narrative beats rather than environmental hazards or faction color-coding typical of open-world games. The trilogy demonstrates escalating stakes through multiplayer tournament framing (AOF2) and geographic expansion (AOF3 to Mexico), communicated through stage selection and new character introductions.
